title: Refinancing Your Home in Greater Vancouver
metaTitle: Mortgage Refinance in Vancouver — When It Makes Sense
metaDescription: Refinance your mortgage in Greater Vancouver to lower payments, take cash out or consolidate debt. Penalty math, break-even analysis, and full-cost comparison.
intro: Refinancing replaces your existing mortgage with a new one — usually at a lower rate, with cash taken out, or both. Done right, it's the most powerful financial move a Greater Vancouver homeowner can make. Done casually, the costs eat the savings.

body.heading: Three reasons people refinance
paragraphs: First: lower the rate. If rates have dropped meaningfully since you signed, refinancing into a new term can save thousands per year. Second: take out cash. Pull equity to fund a renovation, a second-property down payment, business capital, or to consolidate higher-interest debt. Third: extend amortization. Stretching from 20 to 30 years lowers monthly payments — useful if cash flow is tight, expensive in total interest paid.
bullets: Rate-and-term refinance: lower the rate, keep balance the same
Cash-out refinance: increase the balance and put the difference in your pocket
Debt consolidation refinance: roll high-interest debt into the mortgage at sub-5% rates
Amortization extension: stretch the schedule to lower the payment

body.heading: What it costs
paragraphs: If you're breaking your existing term: a prepayment penalty (3 months' interest or IRD, whichever is greater). Plus legal fees ($800–$1,800), an appraisal ($300–$500) and a discharge fee ($250–$400) — often partially or fully covered by the new lender. CMHC default insurance is not available on refinances, so loan-to-value caps at 80%.

Total upfront cost is usually $2,000–$15,000 depending on your old lender and balance. The refinance only makes sense when the savings clearly beat the costs and you'll stay in the mortgage past break-even — typically 18–30 months.

whoItsFor: Have meaningful equity (typically 20%+) and want to access it
Have a current rate at least 0.75% higher than today's market
Want to consolidate higher-interest debt at a lower rate
Are buying a second property and need a down payment
requirements: Recent mortgage statement showing current balance and term
Property appraisal — usually arranged by new lender
Penalty quote from current lender (you request, I review)

faqs.question: How much can I refinance?
faqs.answer: Federally regulated lenders allow refinancing up to 80% loan-to-value. On a $1.5M home with $400k owing, you could in principle borrow up to $1.2M, pulling out $800k of equity (subject to qualifying on the new payment).

faqs.question: Will I be re-stress-tested?
faqs.answer: Yes — at the higher of contract rate plus 2% or the 5.25% federal floor. If your income or debts have changed since the original mortgage, this can affect your maximum new amount.

faqs.question: Is debt consolidation worth refinancing for?
faqs.answer: Often dramatically. Replacing $40,000 of credit-card debt at 22% with mortgage debt at 4.99% saves about $570/month in interest alone. Run that against the penalty and refinance costs to confirm the move pays.
